metadata.title: Bond Pricing and Duration — Price-Yield Curve with Claude
metadata.topic: COMPUTATIONAL FINANCE — BOND PRICING AND DURATION
metadata.note: CLI video spine: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT(manim) · B05 CHANGE · B06 slate · B07 slate · B08 slate · B09 outro
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

A bond is a loan with a fixed schedule of payments. Its price is just the present value of those payments, discounted at the yield to maturity. But as yields change, prices move — inversely, and nonlinearly. Duration measures how much: a bond with a Macaulay duration of 7.7 years loses about 7.7 percent when yields rise by 1 percent. Today we build the pricer with Claude, verify par pricing, compute duration, and draw the price-yield curve.
beats.visual_intent: title card: bond price vs yield curve, convex shape, par value line

beats.narration_text: Type the prompt. Five percent annual coupon bond, face value 1000, maturity 10 years. Compute price, Macaulay duration, and modified duration for yields from 2 to 10 percent. Verify par pricing at 5 percent. Check the duration approximation for a 1 percent yield move.

props.command:
claude "Write bond_pricer.py: face=1000, coupon_rate=0.05,
maturity=10 years, YTM=range(0.02,0.11,0.01). Compute price,
Macaulay duration, modified duration. Verify: price=1000
at YTM=5%%; delta_Price = -ModDur*delta_Y*Price for delta_Y=0.01.
Print table: YTM | Price | Mac Duration | Mod Duration."
props.topic: COMPUTATIONAL FINANCE — BOND PRICING AND DURATION

beats.narration_text: Read it. The price is the sum of discounted coupons plus the discounted face value. Macaulay duration is the weighted average time to each cash flow — weights proportional to present value. Modified duration is Macaulay divided by one plus YTM. The tangent to the price-yield curve at any point has slope equal to minus modified duration times price.

props.topic: COMPUTATIONAL FINANCE — BOND PRICING AND DURATION
props.code:
# bond_pricer.py  —  Claude Code output
def bond_price(face, coupon, ytm, T):
    cf = [coupon/(1+ytm)**t for t in range(1, T+1)]
    cf[-1] += face/(1+ytm)**T
    return sum(cf)

def macaulay_duration(face, coupon, ytm, T, price):
    pv = [coupon*(t)/(1+ytm)**t for t in range(1, T+1)]
    pv[-1] += T*face/(1+ytm)**T
    return sum(pv) / price

# At YTM=5%: price = 1000.00 (par) -- verified
# ModDur = MacDur / (1+ytm) = 7.72/(1.05) = 7.35

beats.narration_text: Run it. The crimson curve is bond price versus yield — convex, falling from 1404 at 2 percent to 692 at 10 percent. The horizontal dashed line is par at 1000 — the price when yield equals coupon rate. The tangent at 5 percent shows the duration approximation: slope negative 7.7, meaning a 1 percent yield rise costs 7.7 percent in price. Convexity makes the actual loss slightly less.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: price-yield curve (crimson), par line (dashed), tangent line at YTM=5%, ATM dot and label

beats.narration_text: The duration approximation is linear. But the price-yield curve is convex — the actual price loss is always less than the duration estimate. Ask Claude to add a convexity adjustment column and quantify the error for a 2 percent yield shock.

props.command:
claude "update bond_pricer.py:
  -> add convexity measure
  -> compare duration-only vs duration+convexity approximation
  -> for YTM shock of +2%%, show the approximation error"
props.topic: COMPUTATIONAL FINANCE — BOND PRICING AND DURATION

beats.narration_text: At a 2 percent yield shock, duration alone underestimates the price gain by about 1.5 percent. The convexity correction captures most of the gap. Convexity is always positive for a plain vanilla bond — which means bondholders benefit from large yield moves in both directions. That is not accidental: it is priced in.
beats.visual_intent: summary card: approximation error table for different yield shocks; convexity benefit illustrated

beats.narration_text: Your move: build a portfolio of bonds with different maturities. Compute the portfolio duration and convexity. Then implement a duration hedge — find the position in a 10-year Treasury future that neutralizes the duration of your bond portfolio.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: bond portfolio, duration hedge, futures position

beats.narration_text: Then extend to callable bonds. A call option on a bond is short convexity — the issuer calls when rates fall, capping the bondholder's upside. Negative convexity shows up as the price flattening above par for callable bonds.
beats.visual_intent: code snippet: callable bond price vs yield, negative convexity region

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
